message.content:
Repo: /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay (branch harden/pass-1, fixes applied vs main). Venv: /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/.venv/bin/python.
The file /tmp/pass1_confirm.json is a JSON array of 22 pass-1 findings (each: issue, title, file, sev, problem, suggested_fix).
Constraints (NOT bugs): local-first/data-sovereign, no metered API, engine imports no adapter, lazy optional backends.

You are independent CONFIRMER #1 of 2 for finding INDEX 7 (0-based) in /tmp/pass1_confirm.json.
Read that item (its issue number, problem, suggested_fix, file). Then VERIFY THE APPLIED FIX on the current branch: read the changed source AND its regression test (git diff vs main, or just read the file + tests), and where useful run a PoC against the venv to confirm the original problem is actually resolved.
Return: appropriate = the fix correctly addresses the root cause in a sound way (not a hack/incomplete patch/over-claim); complete = the fix fully resolves the finding AND has a regression test that would catch a regression. Set either false if not, and say exactly what is missing. Echo the issue number in 'issue'.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll start by reading the finding at index 7.

content.content:
1	[{"issue": "101", "title": "Credential scrubber lets the high-stakes subprocess be redirected off-box (data exfiltration) and switched to a metered provider", "file": "src/assay_engine/reasoning/seam.py", "sev": "HIGH", "problem": "scrubbed_env() (the env passed to the claude_agent_sdk subprocess at line 336) only removes vars whose NAME contains 'KEY' or 'TOKEN' under the ANTHROPIC_ prefix (is_metered_anthropic_credential, lines 113-120). It is a deny-by-name filter, not an allowlist, so every other environment variable is forwarded verbatim to the Claude CLI/Agent SDK subprocess. Multiple Claude Code env vars that contain neither 'KEY' nor 'TOKEN' subvert the two hard invariants this module exists to enforce:

1) DATA SOVEREIGNTY (ADR-0003) bypass: ANTHROPIC_BASE_URL (and ANTHROPIC_BEDROCK_BASE_URL / ANTHROPIC_VERTEX_B", "suggested_fix": "Make the subprocess env a strict allowlist instead of a name-based denylist, OR extend the denylist to strip the provider-redirecting / metered-provider switches. Concretely: also drop ANTHROPIC_BASE_URL, ANTHROPIC_BEDROCK_BASE_URL, ANTHROPIC_VERTEX_BASE_URL, CLAUDE_CODE_USE_BEDROCK, CLAUDE_CODE_USE_VERTEX, CLAUDE_CODE_API_KEY_HELPER, HTTP_PROXY, HTTPS_PROXY, ALL_PROXY (and their lowercase forms),"}, {"issue": "102", "title": "run_json applies retries multiplicatively, causing a retry-storm and unbounded blocking time with no overall deadline", "file": "src/assay_engine/reasoning/seam.py", "sev": "HIGH", "problem": "`run_json` loops `MAX_RETRIES+1` times (line 531) and each iteration calls `_run_with_retries` (line 542), which itself retries up to `MAX_RETRIES` transient attempts AND up to `RATE_LIMIT_MAX_RETRIES` rate-limit attempts. The budgets multiply. PoC (transient errors every attempt): `_attempt` is invoked 9 times = (MAX_RETRIES+1)^2, not the intended ~3. Worse, the rate-limit path: per outer iteration the rate budget sleeps sum(RATE_LIMIT_BACKOFF*(r+1) for r in 0..4) = 450s; times 3 outer iterations = 1350s of pure backoff sleep, PLUS up to (HIGH_STAKES_TIMEOUT+30)=330s of actual work per attemp", "suggested_fix": "Do not nest two retry layers. Either (a) call `_attempt`+`extract_json` directly inside run_json's loop (no inner `_run_with_retries`), or (b) keep `_run_with_retries` and have run_json only re-roll on JSON *parse* failures (not on transient errors the inner loop already handled). Add an absolute wall-clock deadline budget shared across all retries/backoffs so a single call cannot block indefinite"}, {"issue": "103", "title": "CI integration lane runs `pytest --cov` but pytest-cov is not a declared dependency — the lane fails on every run", "file": ".github/workflows/ci.yml", "sev": "HIGH", "problem": "The integration job's final step runs `pytest -q --cov=assay_engine --cov-report=term-missing`. The `--cov` flag is provided by the `pytest-cov` plugin, but pytest-cov appears nowhere in pyproject.toml — not in the `dev` extra (which only pins pytest, ruff, mypy) and not pulled transitively by any heavy extra. The lane installs `.[dev,reasoning,observability,persistence,orchestration,baseline]`, none of which provide pytest-cov or coverage. I verified in a clean Python 3.14 venv: (1) `pip install 'pytest>=8'` does not bring pytest_cov (`importlib.util.find_spec('pytest_cov')` is None); (2) run", "suggested_fix": "Add `pytest-cov>=5` (and, if used directly, `coverage`) to the `dev` optional-dependency group in pyproject.toml so the plugin is installed in both CI lanes. Alternatively, gate `--cov` behind a separate `dev` tool group and confirm it is installed before the integration step. After fixing, the integration lane should be re-run to confirm it passes end-to-end."}, {"issue": "104", "title": "Pool-saturation guard raises PermanentReasoningError for a transient condition, so concurrent callers fail with no retry or backpressure", "file": "src/assay_engine/reasoning/seam.py", "sev": "MEDIUM", "problem": "When all 8 worker slots are in-flight (e.g. 8 concurrent slow/hung backend calls), `_submit_bounded` raises `PermanentReasoningError` (line 153). But `_run_with_retries` catches `PermanentReasoningError` FIRST and re-raises immediately with no retry and no backoff (seam.py:448-449). Saturation is a *temporary* contention/backpressure condition, not a permanent fault, yet it is classified as fatal. PoC: filling all 8 slots with hung calls that time out at 0.2s leaves `_inflight==8`; a subsequent trivial fast call is refused with `PermanentReasoningError("reasoning timeout pool saturated")`. Aft", "suggested_fix": "Raise a retryable signal on saturation instead of `PermanentReasoningError` — e.g. a `RateLimitError`/dedicated backpressure error that `_run_with_retries` retries with bounded backoff — or block with a bounded wait (semaphore acquire with timeout) so callers queue briefly rather than fail. Reserve `PermanentReasoningError` for genuinely unrecoverable saturation (e.g. confirmed-hung slots that nev"}, {"issue": "105", "title": "Postgres ConnectionPool is leaked (never closed) when schema bootstrap fails after the pool opens", "file": "src/assay_engine/persistence/checkpoint.py", "sev": "MEDIUM", "problem": "In `get_checkpointer`, the pool is opened and registered (line 182-190), then schema bootstrap runs inside the try (lines 194-210). If Postgres is reachable but bootstrap fails (missing CREATE privileges, advisory-lock error, a `setup()` DDL error, transient connection drop during DDL), the `except Exception` at line 212 builds a redacted message and re-raises `RuntimeError` — but the already-open pool is NEVER closed. It remains in `_OPEN_POOLS` (so it is only freed at process exit) holding min_size=1 live connection plus a background worker thread. PoC: stubbing `ConnectionPool` so `.connect", "suggested_fix": "In the `except` handler (or a `finally`/try-around-bootstrap), close the pool that was opened in this call before re-raising — e.g. `_safe_close(pool)` and remove it from `_OPEN_POOLS` — so a failed init does not leak the connection/worker thread it just created."}, {"issue": "106", "title": "cosine_similarity_matrix converts the O(n^2) numpy result to a nested Python list, ~70x-ing peak memory and serializing the win", "file": "src/assay_engine/baseline/primitives.py", "sev": "MEDIUM", "problem": "_cosine_matrix_numpy computes the n x n similarity matrix vectorized (good), then at line 112 returns `[[float(v) for v in row] for row in m]` — a full nested Python list of n^2 native float objects. A numpy float64 cell is 8 bytes; a boxed Python float is ~24 bytes plus list-pointer overhead (~8 B) and the per-row list headers, i.e. roughly 32-56 B/cell, ~4-7x the dense array. The conversion is also a pure-Python O(n^2) loop that dominates runtime at scale. PoC against the venv (numpy 2.4.6): n=3000, d=384 (MiniLM-size corpus) took 7.76 s and 390 MB of tracked Python allocation; the returned ", "suggested_fix": "Return the numpy ndarray (or a thin read-only view) from the numpy path instead of boxing to nested lists, letting callers index without the 4-7x blow-up; or expose a streaming/blocked API (e.g. top-k per row, or a generator over row blocks) so the full n x n matrix is never materialized. If a list-of-lists must stay for the pure path's contract, gate the dense materialization behind an explicit `"}, {"issue": "107", "title": "BaselineArtifact deep-freezes its entire contents element-by-element, turning a similarity matrix into a recursively tuple-ized copy", "file": "src/assay_engine/_frozen.py", "sev": "MEDIUM", "problem": "BaselineArtifact.__post_init__ runs freeze_mapping over `contents`, and freeze() recurses through every list/tuple converting it element-by-element into nested tuples (lines 75-76) and hashes every leaf. A study that puts its similarity matrix / embedding matrix (the natural baseline contents) into `contents` as nested Python lists pays a second full O(n_cells) traversal that allocates a parallel nested-tuple copy AND calls hash() on every float. PoC against the venv: freezing a 2000x2000 (4M-cell) matrix into the artifact took 3.29 s and 64 MB on top of the original. Combined with the previou", "suggested_fix": "Do not deep-freeze large numeric payloads cell-by-cell. The freeze() already has array-like handling (lines 85-94) that canonicalizes objects exposing tobytes()/shape to a single (kind, shape, bytes) descriptor in O(1) wrappers — steer builders/contracts toward numpy/array.array for bulk numeric content so this fast path is taken, and/or add a shallow-immutable container for large arrays so conten"}, {"issue": "108", "title": "No supply-chain vulnerability scanning (pip-audit) in CI for a security/privacy-focused production engine", "file": ".github/workflows/ci.yml", "sev": "MEDIUM", "problem": "Neither pyproject.toml nor ci.yml contains any dependency-vulnerability scanning step (`grep -n 'pip-audit\|safety\|bandit'` returns nothing). The project pulls a large, fast-moving transitive surface through its extras — verified by resolving the `observability` extra alone, which installs 150+ packages including cryptography, aiohttp, Flask/Werkzeug, requests, protobuf, grpcio, openai, anthropic, boto3, and SQLAlchemy. With only floor+major-cap ranges (e.g. `cryptography` unconstrained as a transitive, `aiohttp` transitive, `requests` transitive), a clean install on any future day can pull a", "suggested_fix": "Add a CI job (or step in the integration lane) that runs `pip-audit` against the installed environment, failing on known vulnerabilities (with an allowlist file for accepted/unfixable advisories). Consider also committing a constraints/lock file (e.g. `pip-compile` output or `uv.lock`) for the heavy extras so installs are reproducible and audited, while keeping pyproject ranges permissive for libr"}, {"issue": "109", "title": "A raised run leaves a partial provenance trail with no failure entry — the abort reason is unrecoverable from the trail", "file": "/Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/src/assay_engine/pipeline.py", "sev": "MEDIUM", "problem": "GOVERNANCE.md §3 and the docstring at lines 102-103 explicitly promise that "a caller may pass its own trail to run_study so a run that *raises* (a blocked gate, a firewall violation) still leaves an auditable partial trail." That partial trail is auditable for what *succeeded*, but it records NOTHING about the failure itself: when any phase raises (IngestionError, FirewallViolation, PreRegistrationError, GateError, or an adapter exception), control leaves through the bare try/finally at lines 269/522 and the finally block only calls tracker.end_run — it never records a provenance entry for th", "suggested_fix": "Wrap the phase machine body in try/except/finally: on any exception, before re-raising, record a terminal entry e.g. trail.record('run_failed', f'{type(exc).__name__} during {visited[-1].name if visited else "start"}', error_type=type(exc).__name__, message=str(exc), last_phase=visited[-1].name if visited else None). Keep it inside the chain (so verify_records still passes) and guard the record() "}, {"issue": "110", "title": "Failed runs are terminated in the experiment tracker as FINISHED — a failed run is indistinguishable from a successful one in the metric store", "file": "/Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/src/assay_engine/pipeline.py", "sev": "MEDIUM", "problem": "run_study's finally block calls tracker.end_run(run_id) on every exit path, including when a phase raised. MlflowExperimentTracker.end_run calls client.set_terminated(run_id) with no status argument, and MLflow's set_terminated defaults status to "FINISHED" (confirmed from the installed mlflow: the signature is set_terminated(run_id, status=None, ...) and the docstring states it "Defaults to FINISHED"). Result: a run that aborted on a firewall violation or a blocked gate is recorded in the on-box experiment store with status FINISHED, identical to a clean completion. Operators querying the tra", "suggested_fix": "Distinguish success from failure: capture the outcome in run_study (e.g. set an `ok` flag true only after trail.verify() succeeds) and pass a status into end_run. Add a status parameter to ExperimentTracker.end_run / MlflowExperimentTracker.end_run (default 'FINISHED', pass 'FAILED' on the exception path) so set_terminated(run_id, status=status) marks aborted runs FAILED. The set_terminated call a"}, {"issue": "111", "title": "Graph-orchestration gate decisions never enter the hash-chained provenance trail — the documented “append-only trail” for the durable path is unchained, untamper-evident LangGraph state", "file": "/Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/src/assay_engine/orchestration/gatenode.py", "sev": "MEDIUM", "problem": "There are two governance mechanisms (ADR-0010): the synchronous run_study path records every gate decision into the hash-chained ProvenanceTrail (provenance.py), and the durable LangGraph interrupt/resume path (gatenode.make_gate_node) records decisions as plain dicts appended to a LangGraph state list 'gate_decisions'. GOVERNANCE §3 states the chained ProvenanceTrail "is the spine… No step can be retroactively removed or reordered," and GOVERNANCE.md lines 47-50 call the graph's gate_decisions list "the append-only trail." But the graph path's decisions are NEVER written into the chained Prov", "suggested_fix": "Bridge graph gate decisions into the chained trail: have make_gate_node (or a thin wrapper) accept a ProvenanceTrail-backed recorder and record each validated decision via trail.record('gate', ...) at apply time, so the durable path's approvals/rejections land in the same hash-chained, verify_records-checkable trail as the synchronous path. At minimum, reconcile the docs to stop calling the unchai"}, {"issue": "112", "title": "LocalDataVersioner.path_for performs no version-id validation, enabling path traversal outside the store", "file": "src/assay_engine/persistence/versioning.py", "sev": "LOW", "problem": "path_for(digest) builds self._root / digest[:2] / digest with no validation that digest is a hex SHA-256. A caller passing a digest sourced from untrusted data (e.g. a version id round-tripped through provenance records or a model/JSON reply) can escape the store: path_for('../../etc/passwd') -> /tmp/store/../../../etc/passwd, path_for('/etc/passwd') -> /etc/passwd (verified against the venv). LocalDataVersioner and this method are part of the exported public persistence API (persistence/__init__.py), so an adapter that retrieves an artifact by an externally-influenced id can read/return files", "suggested_fix": "Validate the digest before composing the path: reject anything not matching ^[0-9a-f]{64}$ (raise ValueError), or after resolving assert the result is within self._root.resolve() (Path.is_relative_to). Apply the same check in any future getter that maps an id to a filesystem path."}, {"issue": "113", "title": "GateReview is a frozen dataclass whose Mapping payload is not frozen — mutable-in-place and unhashable (issue #7 escape)", "file": "src/assay_engine/pipeline.py", "sev": "LOW", "problem": "GateReview is declared @dataclass(frozen=True, slots=True) with a `payload: Mapping[str, Any]` field but, unlike EVERY other frozen mapping-bearing value record in the engine (ProvenanceEntry, GateDecision, Corpus, ClaimRecord, Verdict, Hypothesis, BaselineArtifact, ReasoningRequest, etc.), it has no __post_init__ that normalizes the field through freeze_mapping. This reproduces both halves of the codebase's own documented issue #7 (see src/assay_engine/_frozen.py module docstring):

1. Mutable-in-place: `frozen=True` only blocks attribute rebinding; the contained dict stays live, so a 'frozen", "suggested_fix": "Add the same __post_init__ used by sibling records, e.g. in pipeline.py:

    from assay_engine._frozen import freeze_mapping  # add to imports

    @dataclass(frozen=True, slots=True)
    class GateReview:
        ...
        payload: Mapping[str, Any]
        def __post_init__(self) -> None:
            object.__setattr__(self, 'payload', freeze_mapping(self.payload))

This makes payload deep-im"}, {"issue": "114", "title": "ProvenanceTrail.record performs a non-atomic check-then-act on shared list state (not thread-safe)", "file": "src/assay_engine/provenance.py", "sev": "LOW", "problem": "record() computes `seq = len(self._entries)` and `prev_hash = self._entries[-1].entry_hash` (read), then does work (clock, digest, freeze), then `self._entries.append(entry)` (write), with no lock. If a single ProvenanceTrail were shared across threads, two concurrent record() calls could read the same seq/prev_hash and append two entries that both claim e.g. seq=N with the same prev_hash, breaking the chain so verify_records() (which asserts e.seq == i and e.prev_hash == prev) would fail — a corrupted append-only audit trail, the exact 'no step can be removed or reordered' guarantee the trail", "suggested_fix": "Guard the read-compute-append sequence with a threading.Lock held across the seq/prev_hash read through the append (or document the trail as single-thread-only and assert it). Given record() also calls an injected clock and a hashing step, hold the lock for the whole method to keep seq/prev_hash/entry_hash mutually consistent."}, {"issue": "115", "title": "Measurement↔Interpretation fence misses dict keys (and non-container objects): an Interpretation can be smuggled into a Measurement", "file": "/Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/src/assay_engine/methodology/fence.py", "sev": "LOW", "problem": "METHODOLOGY.md §6 and the fence.py docstring state the fence "refuses at construction to hold an Interpretation (even nested)" and the Measurement.__post_init__ comment claims it is blocked "at the top level OR nested in a container/metadata (issues #22, #30)." The recursive guard _contains_interpretation does NOT honor that for two cases, both reproducible against the venv:

1) Mapping KEYS. _contains_interpretation recurses only into Mapping VALUES (`any(_contains_interpretation(v) for v in obj.values())`, line 44). An Interpretation is frozen+hashable, so it is a legal dict key. PoC: `Measu", "suggested_fix": "In _contains_interpretation, for the Mapping branch also scan keys: `any(_contains_interpretation(k) or _contains_interpretation(v) for k, v in obj.items())`. That closes case (1) fully and is a small, contained change. For case (2), either (a) tighten the docstrings/METHODOLOGY §6 to state explicitly that the structural guard covers only JSON-native containers and that wrapping judgment in a cust"}, {"issue": "116", "title": "Raw RuntimeError from a shut-down reasoning pool escapes the public ReasoningSeam API un-normalized", "file": "src/assay_engine/reasoning/seam.py", "sev": "LOW", "problem": "`_submit_bounded` calls `_pool.submit(fn)`; if the shared module-global pool has been shut down (which happens during interpreter shutdown, or if any cleanup ever calls `.shutdown()`), `ThreadPoolExecutor.submit` raises `RuntimeError('cannot schedule new futures after shutdown')`. The `except BaseException` at line 160 only decrements `_inflight` and re-raises the raw `RuntimeError` (line 163). It is never normalized to a `ReasoningError`. PoC: after `seam._pool.shutdown(wait=True)`, a call to `_with_timeout(...)` raises a bare `RuntimeError` that escapes `TieredReasoningSeam.run()`. A caller ", "suggested_fix": "Catch the submit-time `RuntimeError` (or convert `BaseException`/`RuntimeError` from submit) into a `PermanentReasoningError`/`ReasoningError` with a clear message (e.g. 'reasoning pool is shut down') before re-raising, so the public seam only ever surfaces the documented error hierarchy."}, {"issue": "117", "title": "QdrantVectorStore owns and never closes its self-constructed client (connection/handle leak)", "file": "src/assay_engine/persistence/vectorstore.py", "sev": "LOW", "problem": "When no client is injected, `QdrantVectorStore.__init__` constructs its own `QdrantClient` via `get_qdrant_client()` (line 64) and stores it on `self._client`, but the class exposes no `close()`/context-manager and never closes the client. `QdrantClient` holds an underlying HTTP connection pool (httpx) / gRPC channel; for a self-constructed client the store is the sole owner, so the connection resources are leaked for the lifetime of every store instance with no deterministic release. Long-running processes that create many short-lived stores accumulate client connection pools.", "suggested_fix": "Make `QdrantVectorStore` a context manager (`__enter__`/`__exit__`) and/or add a `close()` that calls `self._client.close()` — but only for clients it created itself (track an `_owns_client` flag so an injected client, owned by the caller, is not closed)."}, {"issue": "118", "title": "QdrantVectorStore.upsert materializes all PointStructs (with per-vector copies) before sending any batch, so batching bounds payload but not memory", "file": "src/assay_engine/persistence/vectorstore.py", "sev": "LOW", "problem": "upsert builds the complete `points` list — one PointStruct per id, each holding `list(v)` (a fresh copy of the whole vector) — for the entire input before the chunking loop at line 88 sends anything. The comment claims chunking handles 'a large ingest', but it only bounds each HTTP request payload; peak process memory still holds the full corpus of vectors duplicated into PointStructs at once. For a real ingest (e.g. 5M docs x 768 float dims) this is the entire embedding set re-copied into Python objects in RAM simultaneously, which on a single Mac mini node is the OOM path the batch loop appe", "suggested_fix": "Iterate over `zip(ids, vectors)` and build PointStructs one batch at a time inside the loop (or accept an Iterable and pull batch_size items per request), so only one batch of points exists in memory at any moment. Validate len equality lazily or require equal-length up front without forcing full materialization. This makes the batch loop bound memory as well as request size."}, {"issue": "119", "title": "corpus_fingerprint is recomputed redundantly per study (ingest + every build_baseline_artifact call), each a full canonical copy + sort + JSON of the whole corpus", "file": "src/assay_engine/baseline/determinism.py", "sev": "LOW", "problem": "corpus_fingerprint builds a full list-comprehension copy of every unit and relation, sorts all units by id, canonicalizes every attribute mapping via _plain, and json.dumps the whole thing — O(total corpus bytes) time and memory. run_study computes it once at ingest (pipeline.py:280, `cfp`). Then build_baseline_artifact (determinism.py:104) computes it again from scratch for the determinism record's `input_hashes['corpus']` and `corpus_fingerprint`; a study's BaselineBuilder that calls build_baseline_artifact thus repeats the full-corpus canonicalization a second (and the builder may itself fi", "suggested_fix": "Compute corpus_fingerprint once and pass it into build_baseline_artifact (e.g. an optional `corpus_hash` arg) instead of recomputing; or memoize on the Corpus instance (it is frozen/immutable). Threading the already-computed `cfp` from run_study into the determinism harness removes the duplicate full-corpus pass entirely."}, {"issue": "120", "title": "Package version is hardcoded in two independent places (pyproject `version` and `__init__.__version__`), and the second feeds the reproducibility/provenance manifest", "file": "pyproject.toml", "sev": "LOW", "problem": "`pyproject.toml` sets `version = "0.0.1"` (line 7) while `src/assay_engine/__init__.py` separately hardcodes `__version__ = "0.0.1"` (line 28). These are two independent strings with no single source of truth — hatchling is not configured for dynamic versioning (`grep dynamic`/`hatch.version` returns nothing). This is not merely a style nit: `src/assay_engine/baseline/determinism.py` imports `__version__ as _ENGINE_VERSION` (line 24) and stamps it into the determinism/provenance record as `component_versions["engine"]` (line 111), which is part of the hash-chained reproducibility trail. A rele", "suggested_fix": "Single-source the version. Either: (a) set `dynamic = ["version"]` in pyproject and add `[tool.hatch.version] path = "src/assay_engine/__init__.py"` so hatchling reads `__version__` from the module; or (b) drop the literal from `__init__.py` and derive it at runtime via `importlib.metadata.version("assay-engine")`. Option (a) keeps the determinism manifest source authoritative and removes the drif"}, {"issue": "121", "title": "mypy is pinned to `python_version = "3.11"` but CI advertises and matrix-tests up to 3.14, so 3.12–3.14-only type errors are never caught", "file": "pyproject.toml", "sev": "LOW", "problem": "`[tool.mypy] python_version = "3.11"` forces mypy to type-check the code as if running on Python 3.11 regardless of the runner's interpreter. The core CI lane runs mypy across a 3.11–3.14 matrix and the integration lane runs it on 3.12, but because the analysis target is fixed at 3.11, type behavior that differs across versions (e.g. stdlib stubs that gained/changed signatures in 3.12+, version-gated typing constructs) is only ever validated at the 3.11 floor. The project classifies and matrix-tests 3.14 as supported, so the type checker should also exercise the newer targets to make that supp", "suggested_fix": "Either remove the fixed `python_version` so mypy infers the target from the running interpreter (letting the matrix exercise each version), or run an explicit mypy invocation per matrix Python (`mypy --python-version ${{ matrix.python-version }}`). Keep the lazy-backend overrides as-is."}, {"issue": "122", "title": "run_study emits phase/reasoning spans but never starts tracing or stamps run_id baggage — traces cannot be correlated to the run or the experiment-tracker run_id", "file": "/Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/src/assay_engine/pipeline.py", "sev": "LOW", "problem": "run_study is the documented end-to-end composed runner and it opens trace spans for every phase (tracer.span('phase:INGEST') etc.) and the reasoning seam emits LLM/AGENT spans underneath. But run_study never calls bootstrap_tracing() and never wraps execution in run_trace_context(run_id) — grep of pipeline.py shows no reference to either. run_trace_context (tracing.py:145-163) is what stamps the 'assay.run_id' baggage onto every span so traces are correlatable across stores; the orchestration graph runner (graph.py:104-108) does exactly this, but the primary pipeline does not. Consequence: spa", "suggested_fix": "In run_study, after obtaining run_id (or a synthesized correlation id when no tracker is supplied), call bootstrap_tracing() once and wrap the phase machine in `with run_trace_context(corr_id):` so every phase and reasoning span is stamped with the run id and lands at the local collector — mirroring orchestration.graph.run(). Use the experiment_run_id when present so Phoenix spans and the MLflow r"}]

---
 tests/test_baseline.py                             |   27 +

 .../agent-pipeline-deep-audit-wf_82001839-351.js   |  121 +

---SEARCH TESTS---
---FULL CI---
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    services:
      postgres:
        image: postgres:16
        env:
          POSTGRES_PASSWORD: postgres
          POSTGRES_DB: assay
        ports: ["5432:5432"]
        options: >-
          --health-cmd "pg_isready -U postgres"
          --health-interval 5s --health-timeout 5s --health-retries 10
      qdrant:
        image: qdrant/qdrant:v1.12.4
        ports: ["6333:6333"]
    env:
      # loopback host keeps the data-sovereignty guard satisfied; creds are redacted on error
      ASSAY_TEST_POSTGRES_URL: postgresql://postgres:postgres@localhost:5432/assay
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-python@v5
        with:
          python-version: "3.12"
      - name: Install (all extras)
        run: |
          python -m pip install --upgrade pip
          pip install -e ".[dev,reasoning,observability,persistence,orchestration,baseline]"
      - name: Type-check against real backend types (mypy)
        run: mypy
      # Real backends now run: langgraph graph + interrupt/resume, mlflow lifecycle, OTel,
      # numpy primitives, the T3 drain/classification (synthetic SDK, no metered call), AND —
      # via the service containers — the live Postgres checkpointer + Qdrant vector store.
      # Ollama and the metered frontier tier are not in CI and skip honestly.
      - name: Integration + unit tests with coverage
        run: pytest -q --cov=assay_engine --cov-report=term-missing

  # Supply-chain: fail on known-vulnerable dependencies (a security/privacy engine must not ship
  # with advisories). An allowlist file (.pip-audit-ignore, one advisory id per line) covers
  # accepted/unfixable ones. (#108)
  audit:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-python@v5
        with:
          python-version: "3.12"
      - name: Install (all extras) + pip-audit
        run: |
          python -m pip install --upgrade pip pip-audit
          pip install -e ".[dev,all]"
      - name: pip-audit (known-vulnerability scan)
        run: |
          IGNORES=""
          if [ -f .pip-audit-ignore ]; then
            while read -r id; do [ -n "$id" ] && IGNORES="$IGNORES --ignore-vuln $id"; done < .pip-audit-ignore
          fi
          pip-audit --progress-spinner off $IGNORES

content.text:
The fix is fully in place. Let me assess completeness regarding the regression-test requirement, which is the key nuance for a CI/config finding.

The finding (#108) root cause: no dependency-vulnerability scanning anywhere in CI. The fix adds a dedicated `audit` job running `pip-audit` against the full installed environment (`.[dev,all]`), with an optional `.pip-audit-ignore` allowlist mechanism. This directly and soundly addresses the root cause and matches the suggested fix.

On completeness: this is a CI-config finding, not application code, so a pytest "regression test" doesn't apply in the usual sense. The CI job itself IS the regression guard — it runs on every push/PR and fails on any known advisory. The YAML is valid and the job is wired as a real top-level job. The `.pip-audit-ignore` file doesn't exist yet, but the script guards with `[ -f ... ]` so its absence is handled gracefully (audit runs with no ignores), which is correct behavior. The suggested-fix's secondary recommendation (commit a lock/constraints file for reproducible audited installs) was not done, but it was explicitly optional ("Consider also..."), and the core ask — a failing-on-vuln CI scan — is satisfied.

input.reasoning: Finding #108 (no supply-chain vulnerability scanning in CI) is correctly and completely resolved. The fix adds a dedicated top-level `audit` CI job in .github/workflows/ci.yml (lines 74-95) that installs the full dependency surface via `pip install -e ".[dev,all]"` and runs `pip-audit --progress-spinner off` against the installed environment, which fails the lane on any known advisory — exactly the root cause and the primary suggested fix. I validated the YAML parses cleanly (.venv python + PyYAML): jobs are [core, integration, audit] and the audit job has 4 well-formed steps (checkout, setup-python 3.12, install+pip-audit, scan). The `all` extra it installs is defined in pyproject.toml, so the install target resolves. The suggested allowlist mechanism is implemented: the scan step reads `.pip-audit-ignore` (one advisory id per line) into `--ignore-vuln` flags, and is correctly guarded by `[ -f .pip-audit-ignore ]` so the not-yet-created file's absence is handled gracefully (audit simply runs with no ignores). grep confirms pip-audit is now the only/first vuln scanner present and nothing else regressed. This is not a hack or over-claim. On the "regression test" requirement: this is a CI-config finding, not application code, so a pytest regression test is not the applicable guard — the audit job itself IS the continuous regression guard, running on every push/PR and failing on any future vulnerable dependency, which is stronger than a one-off test. The suggested-fix's secondary item (commit a lock/constraints file) was explicitly optional ("Consider also...") and its omission does not block the core guarantee. Constraints respected: no metered API, no off-box behavior introduced.

Summary: The fix correctly and completely addresses the supply-chain scanning gap. A dedicated `audit` CI job was added to `/Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/.github/workflows/ci.yml` (lines 74-95) that runs `pip-audit` against the full installed dependency set and fails on any known advisory, with a graceful optional `.pip-audit-ignore` allowlist. The YAML validates and the install target resolves. As a CI-config finding, the audit job itself is the continuous regression guard (stronger than a one-off pytest test).
